  "opinion": "Dealing with fake content online has been a real pain for me, especially since I spend so much time scrolling through my feeds every day. Sometimes when I see something shared ten times just to make it viral, or when an influencer posts outlandish claims without citing sources, it makes me question what is actually happening in the world.\n\nI've learned that being aware of my own depression plays a big role here because fake news tends to trigger emotional distress. But with my low conscientiousness and moderate neuroticism, I sometimes get lazy or anxious about diving deep into research.\n\nOne thing I do when something feels off—like an emotionally charged post from someone who doesn't seem credible—is delete it after reading unless it's about a hobby like birdwatching where there might be more genuine engagement. response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion:
Dealing with fake content online has been a real pain for me, especially since I spend so much time scrolling through my feeds every day. It's like this constant battle between truth and fiction that affects how I feel about everything. Sometimes when I see something shared ten times just to make it viral, or when an influencer posts outlandish claims without citing sources, it makes me question what is actually happening in the world.

I've learned that being aware of my own depression plays a big role here because fake news tends to trigger emotional distress. The first step I take is always to fact-check anything that sounds too good (or bad) to be true. But with my low conscientiousness and moderate neuroticism, I sometimes get lazy or anxious about diving deep into research.

One thing I do when something feels off—like an emotionally charged post from someone who doesn't seem credible—is delete it after reading unless it's about a hobby like birdwatching where there might be more genuine engagement. Social media is just not worth getting worked up over if you're prone to anxiety anyway. Also, limiting my time on social media helps because less exposure means fewer chances for misinformation poisoning my day.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary:
- Start by being aware of emotional impact due to depression.
- Utilize hobbies as mental escapes from fake content.
- Fact-check suspicious posts when conscientiousness allows.
- Delete content that doesn't add value or cause unnecessary stress.

But I'm not perfect. Sometimes I scroll too much and get caught up in the drama, especially during anxious moments. However, knowing my personality traits helps me reflect on why certain strategies work better than others.
